posts.name: Lonnie F u ll e r
posts.cooked:
I recently opened a Cadence Ciel du Cheval blend. I like Cadence wines. THis one had 12% petit verdot and it punched me in the mouth.

Does petit verdot mellow out over time? I know it’s a common bordeaux blending grape but I thought it was usually used in smaller amounts.
posts.display_username: Lonnie F u ll e r

posts.name: Dave McIsaac
posts.cooked: Try the Briceland PV - you will thank me and Andrew

posts.name: Chris Seiber
posts.cooked:
I don’t know that Cadence wine, and I’m not sure I understand what your reaction to it was.

PV is usually added in very small amounts (<5%) because of its lovely violet aroma. As a stand alone varietal, it makes medium to lighter bodied wine that is, of course, very violet, and maybe with some subtle cab franc type character (gravel, herbs, tobacco). It’s not at all overpowering or highly tannic.

The Briceland version is superb.

posts.name: Steve Costigan
posts.cooked: From what I’ve tried, it’s not all that dissimilar from petite sirah in that it holds its punch longer than the varieties its usually blended with. The early California examples labeled as PV I had were just too tannic/rough but some Washington producers have made some good varitial-labeled PVs.

posts.name: Tom R W  \/\/@rren
posts.cooked:
Last year had a 2008 Gorman The Albatross (Washington), 66% Cab, 34% Petit Verdot. Even at 13yrs old it took 3+hrs in the decanter to mellow out, the tannins are massive.
Fun, interesting wine to drink, but needs lots of age & air, imo.

posts.name: Wes Barton
posts.cooked: PV is a late ripening grape that doesn’t ripen every year at some cooler sites. Take a grape like that and hang too much fruit (overcrop) and it’s going to struggle and not complete every ripening process. So sure, it can make a harsh wine that takes decades to be drinkable, but that’s a choice (or uninformed winemaking).

posts.name: D@vid K@ntr0witz
posts.cooked: Why would you assume the component of the wine that makes up just 12% of the blend is what’s punching you? Have you considered it perhaps is the other 88% that’s doing most of the punching?

posts.name: Otto Forsberg
posts.cooked:
Chris_Seiber:

It’s not at all overpowering or highly tannic.

The Petit Verdot that is often used to add color and tannins to Bordeaux wines (typically made of Cabernet varieties already high in tannins)?

The Petit Verdot Robinson, Vouillamoz et al. describes as making wines that are "powerful, rich, deeply colored, tannic and ageworthy?

The Petit Verdot that Wikipedia describes as “it adds tannin, colour and flavour, in small amounts, to the blend” and Wine-Searcher as “They also have high tannin levels, thanks to the small berries creating a high ratio of skin and seeds to juice.”

That same Petit Verdot is not highly tannic to you? I’m sorry, if I disagree, but no - Petit Verdot might be made into a wine that is not highly tannic, but the variety itself must definitely is highly tannic.

posts.name: Chris Seiber
posts.cooked:
Thanks, Otto. I have had a small number of PV varietal wines and that was my experience, but wines can be made in different styles and from different climates, so I don’t claim to be an expert on all things about the grape.

It would be great to get @Andrew_Morris in the thread.

posts.name: Otto Forsberg
posts.cooked:
Chris_Seiber:

I have had a small number of PV varietal wines and that was my experience, but wines can be made in different styles and from different climates, so I don’t claim to be an expert on all things about the grape.

Indeed, it’s an unreliable to go if one bases views of a certain style or grape on just a few personal experiences. I’ve had a good handful of varietal PV wines, some which have been quite soft and accessible and some which have been tough as old leather boots - not unlike some young Petite Sirah or Tannat varietal wines (varieties which, in turn, can also be made into soft and accessible wines!).

When the topic is on generalizations, I think it’s best not to rely on a few personal data points but at the bare minimum check first what Google has to offer on the topic. That way we can get less confusion and wrong information here, which - I think - is all for the common best.

posts.name: CJ Wiebe
posts.cooked: I really like PV as a stand alone varietal, but as noted by those above, the end product usually ends up being the result of a stylistic choice by the winemaker. Pirramimma makes a great version (White Label) that is nicely balanced and very Bordeaux like, and a number I’ve had from WA state go from restrained wines in all aspects, to more formidable, tannic animals. For whatever it’s worth, I think B. Leighton makes the best PV I’ve ever had.

posts.name: Chris Seiber
posts.cooked:
Otto_Forsberg:

Indeed, it’s an unreliable to go if one bases views of a certain style or grape on just a few personal experiences. I’ve had a good handful of varietal PV wines, some which have been quite soft and accessible and some which have been tough as old leather boots - not unlike some young Petite Sirah or Tannat varietal wines (varieties which, in turn, can also be made into soft and accessible wines!).

When the topic is on generalizations, I think it’s best not to rely on a few personal data points but at the bare minimum check first what Google has to offer on the topic. That way we can get less confusion and wrong information here, which - I think - is all for the common best.

Your posts in this thread seem more than a little harsh for my pretty benignly having shared my experiences about Petit Verdot. Especially considering how agreeable of a tone you normally take on WB. Is there something else that’s been bothering you? Did I give some offense somewhere without realizing it? I’m kind of scratching my head here.

posts.name: Wes Barton
posts.cooked:
If you’re picking it as a blending component, you probably are picking it early. Think the traditional role of a grape in a field blend. There’s a sort of passive practicality that you’d pick some minor component the same time you’re harvesting other grapes. Plus, that suits the role it plays as a minor component. It’s floral aromatics are a valuable aspect, and those are at maximum when grapes aren’t fully ripe. It doesn’t take much in a blend to be impactful at that ripeness level - sometimes the perfect amount, quite distinctive in a blend, is as little as 3%. Depends. But, 12% at that low ripeness level can be way out of whack.

If you pick it as a stand alone wine, you’d likely pick much later. That can yield a soft wine that’s much less complex, not particularly aromatic. There are ways to “solve for that”.

We made a Lodi PV in '13 and consulted with someone making one from the same vineyard this year, as well as someone else who found some hiding in plain sight in a notable vineyard.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: In its origins, Petit Verdot first enters the historical record in the southern Médoc, in what’s today the AOC of Margaux. It was planted in the “palus”, the inferior alluvial terroirs directly adjecent to the Gironde, and then spread inland and to the north. It produces deeply colored wines, but inferior clonal selections meant, for a long time, it was hard to find good material for new plantings. Now there are better clones, and top estates of the Médoc have propagated massale selections of their own. But not all Petit Verdot is created equal, and the genetic material is important. I wouldn’t ever imagine comparing PV with Cabernet Franc, but perhaps an inferior clone cropped at high yields and picked a touch before optimal maturity could be reminiscent of a poor quality Cabernet Franc. It clearly has an interest, but for me not much as a standalone variety.

posts.name: Chris Seiber
posts.cooked:
William_Kelley:

It clearly has an interest, but for me not much as a standalone variety.

What are some you have had which led you to that conclusion? Belle-Vue? Maybe others?

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: You forget that I have lived in California, worked harvest there and even make a bit of wine there today, as well as having spent a bit of time reviewing Washington State for TWA. So I have tasted plenty of North American Petit Verdot as well as the comparatively few Bordelais standalones. I am not going to list wines I have not found thrilling or which have seemed to me admirable blending components but a bit monolithic in their own right, because there seems to be no upside to that.

posts.name: Chris Seiber
posts.cooked:
That makes sense in your position.

I didn’t realize there were that many varietal PV bottlings out there actually. I should look around some more.
